evidence: WebSite missing recommended: potentialAction
ImageObject missing recommended: creator, license
Person missing recommended: jobTitle, sameAs, url, hasCredential
FAQPage schema has 7 Questions but page shows ~13 FAQ pairs

No TL;DR block (expected

or paragraph starting with 'TL;DR:')
No visible 'Last updated' / 'Last reviewed' / 'Next review' stamp on page (schema dateModified alone isn't enough for users or AI)
definitional posts should have DefinedTerm schema — not present
Author has no sameAs URLs (should link LinkedIn + at least one other profile)
Author has no jobTitle or description — credentials not stated
No first-party data signals ("we tested", "our data shows", "we analyzed"). Add at least one concrete observation
Only 3/4 human signals present (author+LinkedIn=false, first-party data=false, original visual=true, 3+ citations=true). High AI-content-flag risk
10 em-dashes (2.76 per 400 words; target <1)
14 tricolons (4.84/500 words; target ≤2)
Specific human voice vs committee 1/10 — Reads as templated marketing content with no individual perspective.
Genuine opinions vs safe claims 1/10 — Every claim is hedged, generic, and safely sourced without any stance.
Unexpected phrasings 5/10 — Magpie metaphor and 'cheapest-to-cite source' are fresh, but most sentences read like template filler.
Specific citations (names, prices, dates) 3/10 — Names engines and one schema type but lacks concrete numbers, prices, or dated findings.
Point of view vs neutral survey 2/10 — Presents balanced explainer without taking a side.
Quotability 1/10 — No line has memorable phrasing worth sharing.
Intro earns attention 2/10 — Opens with boilerplate TL;DR, byline metadata, and a textbook definition—zero hook, reader has no reason to continue.

intro_hook 5/10 — The post's actual opening sentences (after the metadata block) are a dry definitional statement and a table-of-contents promise. Neither creates urgency or surprise. The TL;DR above it is functional but buried in a UI label. The hook needs a concrete stake—a number, a disruption, a tension—to earn the scroll.

specificity 6/10 — The post has one strong specific data point (the Semrush 34% Perplexity citation lift over 90 days) but much of the tactical advice floats in abstract verbs: 'audit,' 'track,' 'optimize,' 'monitor.' The AEO playbook section lists steps without naming a single tool, threshold, cadence, or example brand. The key-takeaways and summary repeat the same abstractions. Concrete nouns—tool names, query examples, specific schema snippet, a named brand outcome—are almost entirely absent outside the one case-study stat.
